annotation_policy: CMT and Koenigsbergian annotations derived from MIPVU-reviewed metaphorical and uncertain lexical units. Each instance cites at least one MIPVU ID with decision_type in METAPHOR_DECISIONS.

entailments: `Vernichtung` extends physical destruction/annihilation to economic, political, or racial elimination of groups
Social or racial elimination is understood through total physical annihilation.
cmt.rival_reading: Physical destruction is extended to social/economic/racial elimination; the basic physical sense underwrites the metaphorical application to groups.
cmt.justification: MIPVU review rule '' marked 1 unit(s) as metaphor-related or uncertain in this sentence.

instances.gloss_notes: German `Vernichtung` denotes total destruction; in Nazi text often applied to enemies or states — may be literal or figurative depending on target.

entailments: `Ehre` frames national reputation or martial obligation as a property that can be given, lost, or restored
National political standing is understood through individual honor as a tangible property.
cmt.rival_reading: Individual honor is extended to national-collective honor as a possessable, transferable property; the Reich's honor can be restored like a physical object.
cmt.justification: MIPVU review rule '' marked 1 unit(s) as metaphor-related or uncertain in this sentence.

instances.gloss_notes: German `Ehre` in political register carries public reputation, martial obligation, and national dignity — not reducible to English honor.

entailments: `Boden`/`Lebensraum` extends physical earth/soil to territorial-organic entitlement — land as the natural living-space of a racial people
Territorial expansion is understood through the organic need for living-space/soil.
cmt.rival_reading: Physical soil is extended to denote an organic territorial right; space becomes a biological necessity for the Volk.
cmt.justification: MIPVU review rule '' marked 1 unit(s) as metaphor-related or uncertain in this sentence.

instances.gloss_notes: German `Boden` in `Lebensraum` contexts extends physical soil into territorial-organic entitlement rhetoric.

entailments: `Schicksal` denotes life circumstances or an assigned lot; the personification may be faded or conventional
Life adversity may be understood through fate as abstract condition or weak personification.
cmt.rival_reading: German `Schicksal` here may be a conventional expression for life-lot rather than a live personification.
cmt.justification: MIPVU review rule '' marked 1 unit(s) as metaphor-related or uncertain in this sentence.

instances.gloss_notes: German `Schicksal` may be literal fate-as-circumstance or personified destiny-as-agent; context distinguishes conventional from live metaphor.
